claims.claim: In the baseline (air-cooled) nail penetration test, all cell thermocouples rapidly exceeded 1000 °C within the first 20 seconds after nail penetration of Cell #1.
claims.quote: The temperature traces in the FIGURE 3 showed that all cell thermocouples rapidly exceeded 1000 °C within the first 20 seconds.

claims.claim: In the baseline (air-cooled) nail penetration test, peak temperatures exceeded the thermocouple measurement range of 1250 °C, with some readings reaching as high as 2200 °C, and the module voltage dropped abruptly to 0 V within a few seconds.
claims.quote: Peak temperatures recorded exceeded the measurement range of the thermocouples (i.e., >1250 °C), with some readings reaching as high as 2200 °C... Voltage readings from FIGURE 5 (A) revealed that the module voltage dropped abruptly to 0 V within a few seconds, confirming total electrical failure of the mini-module.

claims.claim: Under active immersion cooling at 2 LPM and 25 °C fluid temperature, only four thermocouples exceeded 200 °C after nail penetration of Cell #1, and the highest recorded temperature remained below 400 °C, with most cell surface temperatures dropping below 100 °C within 60–90 seconds.
claims.quote: temperature data revealed that only four thermocouples—primarily near Cell #1—exceeded 200 °C. The highest recorded temperature remained below 400 °C. The continuously circulating dielectric fluid rapidly dissipated the heat across the module, and most cell surface temperatures dropped below 100 °C within 60–90 seconds.

claims.claim: Under immersion cooling, the coolant flow rate dropped to near zero after two minutes due to vaporization of the bulk of the coolant following nail penetration.
claims.quote: The flow rate dropped to near zero after two minutes due to vaporization of the bulk of the coolant, as evident in the flow rate curve – observed in FIGURE 5 (B).

claims.claim: In the immersion-cooled test, two of seven cells (Cell #2 and Cell #6) retained near-nominal voltages of 4.1 V and 4.2 V respectively after nail penetration, while no cells retained measurable voltage in the baseline case; the mini-module weight loss was approximately 24.4 grams in the immersion-cooled case versus more than 260 grams in the baseline case.
claims.quote: Post-test disassembly revealed two cells (Cell #2 and Cell #6) with intact casing and retained voltages of 4.1 V and 4.2 V, respectively... TABLE 1 also showed the weight of the mini-module did not change much for the immersion cooled test after the test – around ~24.4 grams, whereas that for the baseline test reduced by more than 260 grams.

claims.claim: During overcharge abuse tests of LTO cells at 100% SOC and 40A (4C), the use of synthetic ester and silicone oil as immersion fluids resulted in strong explosions and severe flame generation from both the battery and the liquids, making them detrimental from a safety perspective.
claims.quote: the use of synthetic ester and silicone oil proved to be detrimental from a safety perspective: in both cases, after the initial overcharge, cell bottom loss, and abundant venting gas release, a strong explosion and severe flame generation from both the battery and the liquids were recorded

claims.claim: During overcharge abuse tests, PFPE170 immersion suppressed battery flames entirely, while PFPE270 only prevented flame propagation but still allowed a small flame on the battery; neither fluorinated fluid has a flash point or fire point.
claims.quote: The tested fluorinated fluids, which have neither a flash point nor fire point, were effective in preventing the propagation of battery flames in the case of PFPE270, and even in suppressing battery flames in the case of PFPE 170.

claims.claim: In single-cell overcharge tests at 100% SOC and 40A, all dielectric fluids delayed thermal runaway onset compared to open-air conditions, with silicone oil providing the largest delay followed by PFPE170, due to enhanced thermal exchange properties of the fluids compared to air.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway onset of the cell was delayed by varying time intervals depending on the fluid adopted, with silicon oil providing the largest delay, followed by PFPE 170. These delays were supported by the enhanced thermal exchange properties of the fluids compared to air.

claims.claim: In the battery pack configuration test, adjacent cells immersed in PFPE170 reached maximum surface temperatures of 62.5°C and 57°C, compared to 160.7°C and 92.3°C for adjacent cells in open-air conditions, demonstrating PFPE170's effectiveness in mitigating thermal propagation to neighboring cells.
claims.quote: In the open-air test, the maximum temperatures recorded on the two adjacent cells were 160.7 °C (T4) and 92.3 °C (T8), respectively. In contrast, during the second test with the cells immersed in PFPE170, the temperatures remained significantly lower and more uniform, being 62.5 °C (T2) and 57 °C (T8).

claims.claim: In single-cell overcharge tests, PFPE170 delayed the time to maximum voltage by 107 s relative to open-air conditions, which was the largest delay among the four fluids tested; silicone oil delayed by 103 s, synthetic ester by 77 s, and PFPE270 by 61 s.
claims.quote: PFPE 170 exhibited the most significant delay of 107 s, followed by silicone oil (103 s), synthetic ester (77 s), and PFPE 270 (61 s).

claims.claim: The minimum cut-off Reynolds number necessary for avoiding thermal runaway was 32 for NCA88 and 234 for NCA91 under forced air convection cooling.
claims.quote: the minimum Recut-off values necessary for avoiding TR were 32 for NCA88 and 234 for NCA91

claims.claim: To avoid thermal runaway in ASSBs, a minimum cut-off Reynolds number of 5000 to 10,000 is required, which is substantially higher than that required for NCA-based LIBs.
claims.quote: to avoid TR in ASSBs, a minimum Recut-off of 5000 ~ 10,000 is required

claims.claim: A 3% increase in nickel content from NCA88 to NCA91 increases the minimum required air velocity for effective battery cooling from 10 cm/s to 73 cm/s, irrespective of SOC.
claims.quote: a minimum air velocity required varied between 10 cm/s to 73 cm/s with an increase of 3 % of nickel content (NCA 88 to NCA91) in cathode composition

claims.claim: For NCA/SCN batteries with nickel content in the range of 85% to 95%, thermal runaway occurs at SOC greater than or equal to 50% irrespective of heating rate.
claims.quote: for NCA/SCN batteries with high range of nickel content (85 % ~ 95 %), the TR occurs at SOC ≥ 50 % irrespective of the heating rate

claims.claim: The critical dimensionless energy parameter delta_critical for NCA88/SCN is 5.75 at 100% SOC, and any value above 5.75 may lead to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the present theory suggests 5.75 as a critical value of dimensionless energy parameter for NCA88/SCN at 100 % SOC. Any value above 5.75 may lead to TR in a cell
